# Analyzing Results from COMP110 Course Survey

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
![Cows in a Meadow](/static/imgs/prettycows.png)

## Figures

![Full Class Assessment of Pace](/static/imgs/fullclass.png)
Rating of pace of instruction across the whole class.

![Likelihood of taking notes vs. pace assessment](/static/imgs/notes.png)
Rating of pace of instruction for those likely to (rated 5-7) or unlikely to (rated 1-4) take notes in class.

![Pace Assessment - STEM Majors](/static/imgs/stem.png)
Ratings of pace of instruction by STEM majors.

![Pace Assessment - Humanities Majors](/static/imgs/humanities.png)
Ratings of pace of instruction by Humanities majors.



## Results

The data analysis provides moderate support for maintaining the current course pace with some minor adjustments. 

The total counts for pacing show clustering around a 4-5 rating, which indicates that the class thinks that the course is moderately paced. However, there are still notable groups in the 6-7 range, suggesting that students feel the course is moving too quickly. 

The distribution suggests that the current pace is generally effective, but still falls short of meeting the needs of many students. 

Data was assessed looking for a correlation between taking notes in class and the pace of the class. When looking at this figure, the distribution is very similar to the whole class. The students who are likely and unlikely to take notes peak at the 4-5 range for pace. 

The next data that was analyzed was the difference between STEM and humanities majors and pacing. The figures show that the peak for STEM majors is at 4, while the peak for humanities is at 5. Though this difference is small in the figure, there are more STEM students in the class than humanities students. Because there are more STEM students than humanities students, the imbalance in population may bias the overall distribution and limit the confidence of conclusions that can be taken from this comparison.


## Conclusion

Based on the results, a good recommendation would be to maintain the current pace but introduce more support and flexibility for the students who feel the lecture is moving too fast. Things such as pre-lecture videos would be a good way to catch students up before they fall behind in class. Things like slow walkthroughs of harder topics, walk-throughs of previous exercises, and a review of exam questions can all help provide a deeper understanding.

To better gauge this data, it would be advantageous to take other aspects of the students into account. Things like prior experience, overall class engagement, and the usage of office hours could alter how you perceive the speed of the course. Collecting data over time could help determine specifically what adjustments should be made in order to improve performance and student satisfaction.

The tradeoffs are minimal but should still be taken into consideration. Increasing the amount of resources the course offers will require more time and effort from the instructor. Some students may feel overwhelmed by the additional work or resources if they are not well-balanced.

Overall, the data suggest that small and targeted adjustments would be more beneficial to the course regarding pacing. The data also suggests that pacing issues aren't strongly tied to note-taking habits or majors; factors like background knowledge or classroom engagement could be a major factor. This would allow for a more effective way of improving learning efficiency and course mastery, all while mitigating negative impacts.
